Neuroprosthesis Data Overview and Processing Pipeline

This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for the FALCON Benchmark M2 dataset, which contains neural recordings from the primary motor cortex in non-human primates during finger movement tasks.
The Finger_RL dataset contains single-neuron recordings from two tetraplegic human participants attempting individual finger movements.
PPC_Finger (human posterior parietal cortex recordings during attempted finger movements)
Data Source: Using adversarial networks to extend brain computer interface decoding accuracy over time (Dryad dataset). Includes data from multiple monkeys across different tasks (ISO/CO/RT/PG).

This project provides a kind of preprocessing Data from: Nonlinear decoding models enable music reconstruction from human auditory cortex activity dataset in the way that raw signals are transformed into signals for model training. The dataset contains intracranial electroencephalogram (ECoG) data recorded from 29 patients with drug-resistant epilepsy while passively listening to Pink Floyd songs. The dataset contains preprocessed neural activity (high-frequency activity, 70-150 Hz), electrode coordinates (MNI template space), and auditory stimuli (raw waveform files, and auditory spectrograms at 32 and 128 frequency points). The HFA and the two auditory spectrograms were sampled at 100 Hz and temporally aligned (190.72 seconds duration).

This dataset is sourced from Zenodo Record 3854034, which contains invasive neural recordings from the primary motor cortex (M1) of non-human primates performing a center-out cursor control task. The neural data were recorded using a 192-channel Utah array, and the behavioral data consist of 2D cursor positions and corresponding target positions. Each .mat file corresponds to one recording session.

Allen Brain Observatory — Visual Coding Neuropixels dataset (NWB, downloaded via AllenSDK). This dataset contains 58 experiments, each with up to 6 Neuropixels probes covering cortical, thalamic, and hippocampal regions, and is designed for systematic study of the mouse visual system under passive stimulation. This workflow uses drifting gratings (8 orientations) as an example, showing the complete process of session selection, unit filtering, spike matrix construction, time binning, and data saving. The official documentation describes the 8 orientations and temporal/spatial frequency settings in detail.
This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for Visual Grating Task ECoG recordings from neurotycho.org.

This project aims to conduct standardized preprocessing on the BOLD5000 dataset, extract fMRI features and perform dimensionality transformation, providing high-quality input features and corresponding labels for subsequent model training related to visual cognition (such as image classification, brain activity prediction, etc.).

This project provides a way to preprocess the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset by transforming raw EEG signals into signals for model training. Only two subsets of the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset were used in the project. The first subset contains only performed left or right hand motor tasks. The second subset contains only imagined left or right hand motor tasks. In both subsets, a total of 103 out of 109 subjects were used, and subjects 38, 88, 89, 92, 100, and 104 were omitted due to data annotation errors.

Based on the requirements of the classification task, we only use the perceptionTraining subset (150 categories × 8 images = 1,200 natural images). This subset has undergone standard fMRI preprocessing in the original paper, including head motion correction, spatial registration, and global normalization, making it suitable for subsequent training-test splitting.
Long-term Recordings of Motor and Premotor Cortical Spiking Activity During Reaching in Monkeys

This project provides a preprocessing method for the THINGS-EEG dataset, transforming raw EEG signals into model-ready data. The dataset contains high-temporal-resolution EEG responses from 10 participants to naturalistic object images, recorded using a Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm.

This project provides A way to preprocess the Data for: A high-performance speech neuroprosthesis dataset to transform raw signals into signals for model training. The dataset consists of 12,100 sentences spoken by a single study participant. We use the "competitionData" version of it, which is a simplified version of the sentence data formatted and partitioned for machine learning research. It contains "train" and "test" partitions for model development, and a reserved "competitionHoldOut" set for the speech decoding competition (labels will be released after the competition). We currently only use the train (8800) versus test (880) partition data. For each sentence, a transcript of what the participant was trying to say was provided, along with a corresponding time series of neural spike activity recorded from 256 microelectrodes in speech related areas of the cortex.

def load_data( dataset_name: str, *, protocol: str = "single_day", task: str | None = None, split_ratio: str = "7:1:2", subject_index: int = 0, day_index: int = 0, seed: int | None = 42, data_dir: Path | None = None, verbose: bool = False, auto_download_missing: bool = True, fallback_to_first_subject: bool = False, repo_id: str = DEFAULT_HF_REPO_ID, repo_type: str = DEFAULT_HF_REPO_TYPE, max_days_per_dataset: int | None = None, ) -> DataLoadResult

load_data is the unified data-loading API in BCIJelly. It automatically handles: Dataset discovery and availability checks (local or auto-download) Protocol/task compatibility validation Single-day or cross-day job construction NPZ reading with task-aware label key selection Train/val/test splitting and feature shape alignment Unified output as DataLoadResult

dataset_name​ Default: none (required). Role: selects which dataset directory to load. Impact: determines all downstream discovery, validation, job construction, and file loading branches. protocol​ Default: single_day. Supported: single_day, cross_day, train-test, train-val-test. Role: defines loading paradigm and split path. Key behavior: train-test and train-val-test run through single-day execution semantics. cross_day builds day-block train/val/test partitions. task​ Default: None, then fallback to classification. Supported: classification, regression, speech. Role: controls label-key selection and label-shape validation. Key behavior: classification expects (N,) labels. regression expects (N,T,2) labels with matching T to neural features. speech currently accepts (N,) or (N,L). split_ratio​ Default: 7:1:2. Role: controls train/val/test ratio. Rules: a:b means train:test. a:b:c means train:val:test. train and test ratios must both be non-zero. Notes: In cross-day, allocation is by day count, not random per-sample split. Under train-val-test, explicit val files may be required by dataset format/declaration. subject_index​ Default: 0. Role: selects subject subdirectory in multi-subject datasets. Notes: For non-multi-subject structures, only 0 is valid. day_index​ Default: 0. Role: selects which day/session job for single-day loading. Notes: For split-files format, only day_index=0 is valid. In cross-day, there is typically one job, so day_index usually stays 0. data_dir​ Default: None (internally falls back to data). Role: overrides dataset root location. auto_download_missing​ Default: True. Role: auto-downloads missing datasets from Hugging Face. fallback_to_first_subject​ Default: False. Role: when subject_index is out of range, fallback to first subject with warning instead of hard error. seed​ Default: 42. Role: controls reproducibility of random splits. Key note: Optional; when omitted, default value 42 is used. verbose​ Default: False. Role: prints dataset/job summaries for diagnostics. repo_id, repo_type​ Role: controls download source (defaults to official HF dataset repo). Typical use: private mirror/testing repository. max_days_per_dataset​ Role: caps discovered/constructed day count. Typical use: fast debugging and reduced-scale experiments.

import bcijelly as bj # single-day classification loaded = bj.load_data( dataset_name="1_FALCONM1-A", protocol="single_day", task="classification", subject_index=0, day_index=0, split_ratio="7:1:2", ) # cross-day classification loaded = bj.load_data( dataset_name="1_FALCONM1-A", protocol="cross_day", task="classification", subject_index=0, split_ratio="7:1:2", ) # single-day regression loaded = bj.load_data( dataset_name="5_Jango_force", protocol="single_day", task="regression", subject_index=0, day_index=0, split_ratio="7:1:2", ) # cross-day regression loaded = bj.load_data( dataset_name="5_Jango_force", protocol="cross_day", task="regression", subject_index=0, split_ratio="7:1:2", )

Main flow in src/bcijelly/dataset/_loading.py: Parameter and capability checks Validate protocol against capability declaration. Validate task (or fallback to classification). Parse split_ratio. Dataset structure discovery discover_dataset_structure identifies format: single_file split_files day_files multi_subject virtual_sessions (for VisualGratingTask) Dataset declaration compatibility validation If dataset_compatibility.json exists, validate requested protocol/task. Subject resolution Resolve subject branch via subject_index. Out-of-range behavior depends on fallback_to_first_subject. Branch A: split_files direct loading Fixed train/test (and optional val) file path flow. train-val-test may require explicit val file. Branch B: build jobs then choose one Build DatasetJob list via build_jobs. For single_day, select by day_index. For cross_day, there is usually one constructed job. Job-level loading (load_job_data) single_day: pre-split single-file keys -> direct train/test load. raw single-file -> split using split_ratio + seed. cross_day: load train/val/test day blocks. align to shared minimal (time, channels) shape. return aggregated test_dataset and per-day test_day_datasets. Return result Unified DataLoadResult fields: train_dataset val_dataset (optional) test_dataset test_day_datasets (common in cross-day) aligned_shape job requested_task If you want to dive deeper, start from: src/bcijelly/dataset/_loading.py src/bcijelly/dataset/_discovery.py src/bcijelly/dataset/_io.py

# dynamic factories (examples) model = bcijelly.model.transformer(task="classification") model = bcijelly.model.lstm(task="regression") # capability query tasks = bcijelly.model.get_supported_tasks("transformer")

bcijelly.model provides dynamic model factory entry points and capability lookup utilities. Main responsibilities: Build model instances by model name + task. Validate model/task compatibility. Expose a unified adapter interface (BaseModel) with train and test methods.
Current factory names: transformer lstm cycle_gan lfads stabilization These names are resolved dynamically by module-level __getattr__.

get_supported_tasks​ def get_supported_tasks(model_name: str) -> tuple[str, ...] Role: return the declared supported tasks for a model. Example outputs: transformer -> ("classification", "regression", "speech") cycle_gan -> ("classification", "regression") create_model​ def create_model(*, model_name: str, task: str) -> BaseModel Role: construct a BaseModel instance after compatibility checks. Usually you call the dynamic factory instead of create_model directly.

transformer: classification, regression, speech lstm: classification, regression, speech cycle_gan: classification, regression lfads: classification, regression stabilization: classification, regression
import bcijelly model = bcijelly.model.transformer(task="classification") print(model.model_name) # transformer print(model.task) # classification
Data loading: load_data.en.md Train API: model.train.en.md Test API: model.test.en.md Summary API: summary.en.md General entry: ../General/overview.en.md
def train(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> dict[str, Any]

model.train is the unified training entrypoint on a model instance. At adapter level it is *args/**kwargs, but the effective parameters are defined by backend model/task implementations. All backends support a concrete train API with named arguments, including train_dataset, val_dataset, seed, epochs, and more.

train_dataset​ Type: tuple, task-dependent. Role: in-memory training data. Required: yes, unless train_datapath is provided. val_dataset​ Type: tuple or None. Role: external validation input. batch_size​ Type: int. Typical defaults: 16, 32, or 64 depending on backend. epochs​ Type: int. Typical defaults: 6 to 400 depending on backend/task. lr​ Type: float. Typical defaults: 1e-4, 1e-3, 4e-3, etc. weight_decay​ Type: float. Role: optimizer regularization. seed​ Type: int. Default in all current backends: 42. device​ Type: str. Typical values: "auto", "cpu", "cuda". Role: runtime device selection. patience​ Type: int. Role: early-stop patience behavior where implemented. run_name​ Role: labels run outputs directory/name. output_dir​ Type: str. Default: "./outputs" (all current backends). Role: output root directory. log_csv​ Type: str | None. Default: None (all current backends). Role: optional custom CSV log path. save_ckpt​ Type: bool. Default: usually True. Exceptions: speech backends default to False: src/bcijelly/model/_lstm/speech.py src/bcijelly/model/_transformer/speech.py Role: whether to save checkpoints. plot​ Type: bool. Default: False (all current backends). Role: whether to generate training diagnostic plots. verbose​ Type: bool. Default: False (all current backends). Role: print extra runtime logs. train_datapath​ Type: str | None. Role: path-based training input (npz flow). Required: yes, if train_dataset is not provided. val_datapath​ Type: str | None. Role: path-based validation input. val_ratio​ Type: float. Role: internal validation split ratio for backends that support internal split.

Classification/Regression backends usually expect: train_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray] val_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray] Speech backends expect 5-tuples: train_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray] val_dataset: tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray, np.ndarray]

Transformer (classification/regression)​ num_layers: int d_model: int n_heads: int dropout: float Transformer (speech)​ Same core Transformer params plus: kernel_len: int stride_len: int gaussian_smooth_width: float white_noise_sd: float constant_offset_sd: float use_day_idx: bool LSTM (classification/regression)​ num_layers: int hidden_size: int dropout: float bidirectional: bool pooling: str (classifier backend) LSTM (speech)​ Same LSTM core params plus: kernel_len: int stride_len: int gaussian_smooth_width: float white_noise_sd: float constant_offset_sd: float use_day_idx: bool LFADS​ fac_dim: int dropout: float Regressor-only: reduce: str = "center" CycleGAN​ decoder_epochs: int decoder_lr: float g_lr: float d_lr: float source_ratio: float generator_hidden: int discriminator_hidden: int dropout_decoder: float dropout_g: float dropout_d: float identity_weight: float cycle_weight: float loss_type: str calib_ratio: float min_calib_trials: int max_calib_trials: int | None enable_alignment: bool Stabilization​ baseline_ntrials: int n_latents: int fa_n_restarts: int fa_ll_thresh: float fa_max_its: int fa_min_priv_var: float align_th: float align_n: int Classifier backend: pooling: str hidden_size: int enable_alignment: bool Regressor backend: hidden_sizes: Sequence[int] | None fine_tune: bool fine_tune_epochs: int

train_dataset and train_datapath are alternative input channels; at least one must be provided. External validation is enabled when val_dataset or val_datapath is provided. Some backends use val_ratio for internal split, while others require explicit external validation. After backend train(...) returns, adapter state can be captured for later model.test(...) reuse.
Type: dict[str, Any] Typical fields include training/validation metrics and run metadata (for example device/run directory). Exact keys are backend-dependent.
train_result = model.train( train_dataset=loaded_data.train_dataset, val_dataset=loaded_data.val_dataset, device="auto", run_name="demo_train", epochs=1, verbose=False, )

def summary( loaded_data: Any | None = None, model: Any | None = None, train_result: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, test_result: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, title: str = "Summary", mode: Literal["compact", "full"] = "compact", ) -> dict[str, Any]
summary builds, prints, and returns a user-facing summary from core pipeline results. It can produce: A compact overview for quick inspection. A full sectioned output for detailed diagnostics.

loaded_data​ Default: None. Role: output from bcijelly.load_data, used to extract dataset/job/shape metadata. model​ Default: None. Role: model instance used to infer module name, task type, and primary metric hints. train_result​ Default: None. Role: dictionary returned by model.train. test_result​ Default: None. Role: dictionary returned by model.test. title​ Default: Summary. Role: display title for compact print mode. mode​ Default: compact. Supported: compact, full. Role: controls printed detail level.

summary(...) returns a dictionary with fixed top-level sections: compact: key pipeline highlights. loaded_data: expanded load/job/shape details. module: model capability metadata. train_result: copied training result dictionary. test_result: copied testing result dictionary.

Summary metric display follows this order: Prefer model-declared PRIMARY_TEST_METRIC if present in test_result. Fallback scan of common keys: acc, r2, mse, mae, loss. For compact mode, metric row is shown in a merged text form similar to: acc: train ..., val ..., test ... or r2: train ..., val ..., test ...

compact_summary = bcijelly.summary( loaded_data=loaded_data, model=model, train_result=train_result, test_result=test_result, mode="compact", ) full_summary = bcijelly.summary( loaded_data=loaded_data, model=model, train_result=train_result, test_result=test_result, mode="full", )

Species: Macaca mulatta (Rhesus monkey) Number of subjects: Multiple
Recording Modalities: Simultaneous acquisition of Electrocorticography (ECoG) and eye-tracking data during task performance. No motion tracking was conducted.

Task Type: Passive Viewing Task. Stimuli: Six distinct movie clips. Subject State: The subject (macaque) remained seated in a head-fixed configuration to minimize motion artifacts and ensure the stability of visual stimulation. Feedback & Reward Mechanism: No food reward was provided. The subject was not required to perform any active behavioral interactions, thereby eliminating potential confounding effects from operant conditioning. Synchronized Data Acquisition: Neural Recording: Cortical ECoG signals were recorded at a sampling rate of 1 kHz. Behavioral Recording: Eye-tracking data were acquired at a sampling rate of 120 Hz. Synchronization & Constraints: Neural and behavioral signals were strictly synchronized via hardware/software triggers. The experimental design explicitly excluded limb motion tracking, focusing the analytical scope on the mapping between visual input, oculomotor behavior, and cortical neural dynamics.
This workflow processes 128-channel ECoG LFP data and converts it into a binned spike representation suitable for classification and pattern recognition tasks.

import bcijelly as bj loaded_data = bj.load_data( dataset_name="13_Emotion", protocol="single_day", # single_day | cross_day | ... task="classification", subject_index=0, day_index=0, # Invalid parameter when cross_day is enabled split_ratio="7:1:2", verbose=False, ) train_dataset, val_dataset, test_dataset = loaded_data.train_dataset, loaded_data.val_dataset, loaded_data.test_dataset train_neural_signal, train_label = train_dataset val_neural_signal, val_label = val_dataset test_neural_signal, test_label = test_dataset print(train_neural_signal.shape, train_label.shape) print(val_neural_signal.shape, val_label.shape) print(test_neural_signal.shape, test_label.shape)

Filter cutoff frequencies: low cutoff > 0 Hz, high cutoff < Nyquist frequency Trial truncation: trials shorter than trial_duration_sec are discarded bin_size must evenly divide target_timesteps or the remainder is discarded Detection sensitivity: 2σ can be adjusted to 1.5σ or 3σ Refractory period: refractory_ms is measured in timesteps after downsampling, ensure proper conversion

This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for the FALCON Benchmark M2 dataset, which contains neural recordings from the primary motor cortex in non-human primates during finger movement tasks. The goal is to transform raw .nwb spike train data into synchronized, windowed samples of neural activity and behavioral labels (e.g. hand position or velocity) suitable for training machine learning models.
Species: Macaca mulatta (Rhesus monkey) Number of subjects: 1
Recording Location: Neural activity was recorded using an electrode array implanted in the primary motor cortex (M1).

Experimental Task: Subjects moved two groups of fingers in response to visual target cues presented on a screen. Finger position data, derived from the state of the manipulator, is provided and has been normalized between full flexion and full extension.

import bcijelly as bj loaded_data = bj.load_data( dataset_name="2_FALCON M2", protocol="single_day", # single_day | cross_day | ... task="regression", subject_index=0, day_index=0, # Invalid parameter when cross_day is enabled split_ratio="7:1:2", verbose=False, ) train_dataset, val_dataset, test_dataset = loaded_data.train_dataset, loaded_data.val_dataset, loaded_data.test_dataset train_neural_signal, train_label = train_dataset val_neural_signal, val_label = val_dataset test_neural_signal, test_label = test_dataset print(train_neural_signal.shape, train_label.shape) print(val_neural_signal.shape, val_label.shape) print(test_neural_signal.shape, test_label.shape)

Can't read .nwb files: Ensure files are complete and follow NWB v2.0+ spec Shape mismatch: Verify behavioral data aligns with binned spike data Need customization?: Reach out to the maintainer for help with modifications or enhancements Default spike bin size is 20ms; you may change bin_size in bin_spikes_for_regression() Choose window_size and stride based on modeling requirements and dataset length Behavioral data sampling rate is 50 Hz; spike times are aligned accordingly Trials shorter than the window size will be skipped

Species: Macaca mulatta (Rhesus monkey) Number of subjects: 3 (each dataset split counted as one “subject” in DANDI; actual recordings from one monkey for M1-A, additional monkey data here)

Neural Input: Multi-unit activity (MUA) captured from the primary motor cortex (M1) using chronically implanted electrode arrays. Muscle Output: 16-channel intramuscular EMG (iEMG) signals, synchronized with neural data to capture the activation patterns of upper-limb muscles during task execution.

Type: Reach-to-grasp, center-out reaching task. Conditions: 4 different objects 8 possible target positions Behavioral events recorded: gocue_time move_onset_time contact_time reward_time result, number, tgt_loc, tgt_obj, obj_id, condition_id

import bcijelly as bj loaded_data = bj.load_data( dataset_name="1_FALCON M1-A", protocol="single_day", # single_day | cross_day | ... task="classification", subject_index=0, day_index=0, # Invalid parameter when cross_day is enabled split_ratio="7:1:2", verbose=False, ) train_dataset, val_dataset, test_dataset = loaded_data.train_dataset, loaded_data.val_dataset, loaded_data.test_dataset train_neural_signal, train_label = train_dataset val_neural_signal, val_label = val_dataset test_neural_signal, test_label = test_dataset print(train_neural_signal.shape, train_label.shape) print(val_neural_signal.shape, val_label.shape) print(test_neural_signal.shape, test_label.shape)

The Finger_RL dataset contains single-neuron recordings from two tetraplegic human participants attempting individual finger movements.

Core Brain Regions: Left Posterior Parietal Cortex (PPC, area PC-IP): Responsible for extracting high-level motor intentions, spatial mapping, and cognitive planning signals. Left Motor Cortex hand knob (MC hand knob): Targeted for recording primary motor execution signals specifically associated with fine hand motor control. Neural Sensing: Captures discrete neuronal spike activity (single-unit activity) via chronically implanted intracortical microelectrode arrays.
Task: Attempted individual finger movements (both hands)

import bcijelly as bj loaded_data = bj.load_data( dataset_name="3_Finger Rl", protocol="single_day", # single_day | cross_day | ... task="classification", subject_index=0, day_index=0, # Invalid parameter when cross_day is enabled split_ratio="7:1:2", verbose=False, ) train_dataset, val_dataset, test_dataset = loaded_data.train_dataset, loaded_data.val_dataset, loaded_data.test_dataset train_neural_signal, train_label = train_dataset val_neural_signal, val_label = val_dataset test_neural_signal, test_label = test_dataset print(train_neural_signal.shape, train_label.shape) print(val_neural_signal.shape, val_label.shape) print(test_neural_signal.shape, test_label.shape)

PPC_Finger (human posterior parietal cortex recordings during attempted finger movements)
Species: Human (Homo sapiens) Subjects: Single tetraplegic human participant

Recording Method: Electrical physiological signals were collected using a 96-channel Utah array (Blackrock Microsystems) implanted in the left posterior parietal cortex (PPC, PC-IP region) for electrophysiological recordings. Allen Brain Observatory — Visual Coding Neuropixels dataset (NWB, downloaded via AllenSDK). This dataset contains 58 experiments, each with up to 6 Neuropixels probes covering cortical, thalamic, and hippocampal regions, and is designed for systematic study of the mouse visual system under passive stimulation. This workflow uses drifting gratings (8 orientations) as an example, showing the complete process of session selection, unit filtering, spike matrix construction, time binning, and data saving. The official documentation describes the 8 orientations and temporal/spatial frequency settings in detail.
Species: Mouse (Mus musculus) Number of subjects: Multiple
Recording Sites: Spanning multiple brain regions including the cortex, thalamus, and hippocampus

Standardized Stimulation Protocol: A standardized visual stimulation protocol was administered to each subject (mouse) to ensure temporal reproducibility across experiments and to facilitate the parallel mapping of visual responses across diverse brain regions. Characterization of Classical Stimulus Features: Stimulus Content: Comprised of Gabor patches, full-field drifting gratings, and moving dots. Experimental Objective: Aimed at precisely characterizing the tuning properties of individual neurons, such as orientation selectivity or spatial frequency preference. Ecologically Relevant Experiments: Stimulus Content: A series of complex natural images and natural movies were presented. Experimental Objective: To explore the synergistic interactions and population dynamics across different visual cortical areas within an ecologically relevant and realistic context.

This project provides a complete preprocessing pipeline for Visual Grating Task ECoG recordings from neurotycho.org. The goal is to extract event-related segments from raw .mat files, perform preprocessing (e.g., common average referencing (CAR), downsampling), and save structured datasets suitable for neural decoding experiments. Reference: Neural Decoding Using Kernel-Based Functional Representation of ECoG Recordings
Species:Rhesus macaque (Macaca mulatta) Number of subjects:Multiple
The experiment synchronously recorded the subject’s cortical electroencephalography (ECoG) data and eye position information using a 128-channel implanted microelectrode array.

Experimental Task: Subjects were head-fixed with both arms restrained. ECoG signals and eye positions were recorded simultaneously. Visual Stimulation: A monitor was placed in front of the monkey, displaying a grating pattern moving in eight different directions. Stimulus events are encoded as analog values and can be decoded from the 129th channel. Trial Structure: The monkey was not required to fixate on the screen. Blank screens and stimulus patterns alternated every 2 seconds. Physical Parameters: The sinusoidal grating had a spatial period of 27 mm and moved at a velocity of 108 mm/s (4 Hz). The distance between the monkey and the screen was 490 mm.

This project aims to conduct standardized preprocessing on the BOLD5000 dataset, extract fMRI features and perform dimensionality transformation, providing high-quality input features and corresponding labels for subsequent model training related to visual cognition (such as image classification, brain activity prediction, etc.).
Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging (fMRI) records the temporal distribution of BOLD signal intensity within multiple visually relevant regions of interest (ROIs: PPA, RSC, OPA, LOC, EarlyVis).

Visual Stimulus Task: In a single trial, a visual stimulus image is presented to the participant, with each image corresponding to a regressor (1s ON, 9s OFF). The experimental images are sourced from three categories/datasets: ImageNet, COCO, and Scenes (SUN). Each fMRI run consists of 37 visual stimulus images.
The processed final data is stored in .npz format files, which can be directly read by Python and deep learning frameworks such as PyTorch/TensorFlow.

The data is stored on a per-participant basis. After loading the .npz file using np.load, the following two keys can be used to access the data: fmri: This represents the 2D fMRI image feature data, with a shape of (trials, 16, 16). label: This corresponds to the target label data, containing three possible numerical values (with an overall distribution ratio of approximately 2:2:1): 0: COCO category 1: ImageNet category 2: Scenes category

This pipeline is designed to convert fMRI time-series data into a 2D feature tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading and preprocessing to the construction of 2D features and labels. 1. Data Loading and Parsing The voxel time-series data from the ROIs, after GLM analysis, is directly obtained from the BOLD5000 Release 2.0 dataset. The data is stored on a per-participant basis. The .npz format files are loaded using the np.load function, and the corresponding data matrices are parsed using the fmri and label keywords. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Segmentation The raw fMRI data undergoes preprocessing via fMRIPREP, which includes motion correction and spatial normalization, followed by the estimation of mean-centered β coefficients using a GLM single-trial model. The extracted complete time-series is then segmented into three parts based on the dataset of the images presented in each trial. Linear interpolation is applied to the segmented sequences to adjust them to a uniform length. 3. Feature Extraction & Transformation The Gramian Angular Field (GAF) method is employed to convert the 1D time-series data into a 2D matrix. Using the mark_img function, the GramianAngularField algorithm from the pyts.image library is applied to the time-series features (after removing the label column, dat.iloc[:,:-1]). Normalization and polar coordinate transformation (e.g., summation calculation) are performed, and the resulting image data is reshaped into a single-channel tensor format suitable for CNN input, with a shape of (samples, 16, 16, 1). 4. Label Generation & Mapping The ImgType field from the corresponding events.tsv file is extracted to generate labels. These labels are standardized by grouping repeated and non-repeated images into a single class. The string labels are then converted into numerical format for machine learning, extracted as dat['label'], and finally mapped into three discrete values: 0 (COCO), 1 (ImageNet), and 2 (Scenes). The data distribution ratio is maintained at approximately 2:2:1.

Technique: Mesoscopic single-photon / wide-field calcium imaging, used to record fluorescent signals from the cortex of head-fixed mice. The spatial resolution of the images is adjusted to 128 × 128 pixels.

Neural Activity and Behavior Synchronization: Neural activity and behavioral performance are synchronously recorded to analyze the relationship between cortical dynamics and behavior. Behavior Definition: Binary labeling based on the mouse’s movement speed: frames with movement speed > 0.5 cm/s are defined as “running” state, while frames with movement speed < 0.5 cm/s are defined as “resting” state. Experiment Scale: A total of 64 experimental sessions, with each session lasting 10 minutes. The number of sessions for the 5 mice are 11, 12, 14, 15, and 12 respectively.
The calcium imaging frame rate is 30 frames per second (30 fps). Each 10-minute experimental session generates approximately 18,000 frames of image data.

Imaging Data: .tif (raw images) Behavioral Labels: .mat Mapping Configuration: .csv Statistical Parameters: .npy (mean and standard deviation) Preprocessed Data: .npz (for direct loading and reading by deep learning models)

This pipeline is designed to convert raw wide-field calcium imaging data and corresponding behavioral labels into a standardized time-series window format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading, temporal alignment, feature normalization to windowing and saving the sequence. 1. Data Loading & Temporal Alignment The .csv configuration file is read to obtain the mapping between data IDs and mouse numbers, and the corresponding behavioral sequence (run/rest) labels are extracted from the .mat file. The tifffile library is used to load the raw wide-field calcium imaging data in .tif format. A tolerance-matching algorithm (allowing ±5 frame errors) is applied to handle mismatched lengths (truncation or padding), ensuring strict alignment between the number of image frames and the behavioral label sequence. 2. Frame-wise Standardization & Dimensionality Reduction The precomputed per-pixel mean and standard deviation for each experimental phase are loaded from a file. Each aligned frame of the image is processed with per-pixel standardization. Based on the setting (e.g., flatten=True), the standardized single-frame images are flattened along the spatial dimension, and the channel-wise average is taken, reducing the data to a one-dimensional feature vector. 3. Sequence Sliding Windowing A fixed time window length (T=30, i.e., 30 consecutive frames as one sample) is set, and the continuous feature sequence is sliced into sliding windows with a stride of 1, generating all possible continuous time-window samples. 4. Label Generation & Formatting Storage For each sliced time window, the global label of the window is determined by majority voting (i.e., the most frequent label) among all the individual frame labels contained within that window. Finally, the windowed feature tensor (calcium) and the aligned label array (label) are packaged, named according to the convention YYYYMMDD_Mouse#.npz, and saved in .npz format for direct loading and training by the model.

This project provides a way to preprocess the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset by transforming raw EEG signals into signals for model training. Only two subsets of the EEG Motor Movement/Imagery dataset were used in the project. The first subset contains only performed left or right hand motor tasks. The second subset contains only imagined left or right hand motor tasks. In both subsets, a total of 103 out of 109 subjects were used, and subjects 38, 88, 89, 92, 100, and 104 were omitted due to data annotation errors.

The BCI2000 system was used to record 64-channel EEG signals. The physical electrode layout strictly follows the international 10-10 system (excluding peripheral or specific electrodes such as Nz, F9, F10, FT9, FT10, A1, A2, TP9, TP10, P9, and P10).

The experiment primarily focuses on Motor Execution and Motor Imagery. Participants are required to respond according to target cues displayed on the screen. Each participant must complete 14 experimental runs, which include: Baseline state: 2 runs of 1 minute each (eyes closed resting, eyes open resting). Task state: 12 runs of 2 minutes each (the following 4 tasks are repeated 3 times): Task 1: Execute opening and closing of the left or right fist based on left-right cues displayed on the screen. Task 2: Imagine opening and closing the left or right fist based on left-right cues displayed on the screen. Task 3: Execute opening and closing of both fists (up) or both feet (down) based on up-down cues displayed on the screen. Task 4: Imagine opening and closing both fists (up) or both feet (down) based on up-down cues displayed on the screen.
160 Hz (i.e., 160 data samples are collected per second per channel).

Original Data Format: The data is provided in EDF+ format (containing 64 EEG signal channels and 1 event annotation channel), accompanied by an .event annotation file for parsing. Preprocessed Format: After filtering and segmenting, the data is outputted in the commonly used NumPy array format for machine learning, and saved in a compressed .npz file (e.g., EMM_task1_preprocessed.npz) for easy direct use in code.

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG recording data into a standardized tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading to feature and label construction. 1. Data Loading The core function load_data() is used to directly read and load the 64-channel raw EEG experimental data from 109 volunteers, recorded by the BCI2000 system, from the local folder. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Chunking A notch filter is applied to the EEG signal to eliminate 60 Hz power line noise, followed by band-pass filtering between 2 Hz and 60 Hz. The experimental data is then strictly truncated into 4-second segments (at 160 Hz sampling rate, resulting in 640 samples). Using a sliding window method, the data is further divided into 8 non-overlapping time segments (each window contains 80 samples). 3. Feature Extraction Features are extracted from the filtered and chunked data, and formatted into a NumPy array that can be processed by both CPU and GPU. A feature matrix with the shape (number of samples, number of channels, number of time points) is constructed and saved in .npz format to the output folder. 4. Label Generation & Mapping Each segmented window is precisely mapped to its corresponding experimental condition, receiving the same target labels as the original trials (e.g., representing the type of movement and whether the movement is for the left or right hand). The final supervised label array is generated and aligned with the shape (number of samples, labels).

Based on the requirements of the classification task, we only use the perceptionTraining subset (150 categories × 8 images = 1,200 natural images). This subset has undergone standard fMRI preprocessing in the original paper, including head motion correction, spatial registration, and global normalization, making it suitable for subsequent training-test splitting.

fMRI (Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging): Each scanning session includes: Functional images (EPI): Covering the entire brain. Anatomical images (in-plane T2 weighted): Acquired using the same slices as the EPI. High-resolution anatomical reference images (T1 weighted): Scanned only once for each participant, used as structural references.

The experiment is divided into two main types of tasks: Image Presentation (Perception) and Imagery (Representation) tasks. Image Presentation Task (Perception Task): Participants view object images from ImageNet and perform a 1-back image repetition task. Training Set (perceptionTraining): Contains 150 object categories, with 8 images per category, totaling 1,200 natural images. Each image is presented only once (24 runs in total, with 3-5 sessions). Test Set (perceptionTest): Contains 50 object categories, with 1 image per category, totaling 50 images. Each image is repeated 35 times (35 runs in total, with 4-6 sessions). Imagery Task (imageryTest): Participants are asked to mentally visualize one of the 50 categories from the test set. There are 20 runs in total (3-5 scanning sessions), with each run containing 25 categories, and 10 samples per category. Participants are also required to self-rate the vividness of their mental imagery.

MRI Imaging Data: All DICOM files were converted to NIfTI-1 (.nii.gz) format using FreeSurfer’s mri_convert. The T1-weighted images have been processed with pydeface to remove facial features. Task Event Files: In .tsv format, containing detailed stimulus timestamps and behavioral records. ROI Masks: Binary mask images in .nii.gz format.

The dataset is organized at the participant level and contains the following main directories/session types: ses-perceptionTraining: fMRI data for the perception training set. ses-perceptionTest: fMRI data for the perception test set. ses-imageryTest: fMRI data for the imagery test task. ses-anatomy: T1-weighted anatomical reference images. sourcedata: Manually defined Regions of Interest (ROI) mask files. derivatives/preproc-spm: fMRI data preprocessed using SPM.

This pipeline is designed to convert preprocessed 4D fMRI time-series data into a reduced-dimensional feature tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading, extraction, alignment, feature engineering, to the final construction of the data structure. 1. Data Loading & Indexing Preprocessed 4D fMRI data (.nii.gz), predefined ROI mask files (.nii.gz), and event annotation files (.tsv) are obtained from the derivatives folder. The data is strictly organized by participant (subject), session, and run levels. A data index dictionary is built using regular expression matching to map participants to their corresponding data files. 2. Signal Extraction & Temporal Alignment Each participant’s ROI mask is loaded and binarized. The 2D voxel signal matrices for each ROI are extracted in a fixed order. Based on the fMRI sampling period (TR = 3.0 s) and the peak latency of the Hemodynamic Response Function (HRF), the stimulus onset is shifted by one TR for temporal alignment. Voxel signals for each trial corresponding to the aligned TRs are extracted and concatenated to form trial data with a shape of [total_voxels, TRs_per_trial]. The trial labels are standardized to integer encoding starting from 0. 3. Feature Engineering & Dimensionality Reduction The data for each trial at each time point (TR) is standardized using the StandardScaler, such that the data has a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1. Subsequently, Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is applied to reduce the dimensionality for each time point, and the results are integrated across all time points. The final feature matrix is constructed while preserving the principal component matrix and the proportion of explained variance. 4. Label Generation & Data Storage The processed data is stored by participant in .npz format. After loading the data with np.load, the feature matrix can be extracted using the fmri keyword with a shape of (trials, number_of_principal_components, TRs). Labels ranging from 0 to 149 (150 possible values) can be extracted using the label keyword. Additionally, specific label names, ROI information, and voxel count statistics can be accessed through label_names, rois, and roi_voxel_counts.

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG recording data into a standardized tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from signal preprocessing, feature extraction, to label alignment. 1. Data Loading The load_data() function is used to traverse the specified directory by session ID (session_id) and participant ID (sub_id), directly reading and loading the data files stored in .npz format. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Segmentation The raw EEG signals are downsampled to a unified sampling rate of 200 Hz, and a 1 Hz - 75 Hz band-pass filter is applied to remove environmental noise and physiological artifacts. The continuous EEG signal is then strictly segmented into 4-second non-overlapping time segments, with each segment treated as an independent data sample. 3. Feature Extraction & Smoothing From each segmented 4-second segment, power spectral density (PSD) and differential entropy (DE) features are extracted for five classical frequency bands (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma). Then, a moving average or linear dynamic system (LDS) filtering method is applied to smooth the features, further reducing noise. The final result is a three-dimensional feature tensor (features) with dimensions corresponding to the number of samples, number of channels, and frequency band feature dimensions. 4. Label Generation & Synchronization The emotional categories (happiness, sadness, fear, neutral) induced by the movie clips viewed by the participant in each trial are precisely mapped to discrete supervised labels. The time windows of the data are strictly aligned with the labels to ensure that each 4-second feature segment has a corresponding emotional ground truth, resulting in the final synchronized one-dimensional label array (labels).

Participants: 20 participants (10 male, 10 female), aged 19-26 years (mean age = 22.5 years, standard deviation = 1.8). Conditions: All participants are right-handed, with normal or corrected-to-normal vision and normal hearing. Screening: Participants were screened using the Eysenck Personality Questionnaire (EPQ).
EEG (Electroencephalography): EEG signals were recorded using a 62-channel Neuroscan system. Eye-tracking: Eye movement parameters were synchronously recorded using the Tobii Pro Fusion eye tracker.

The emotion induction is based on 80 video clips (12 clips for each of the following emotions: happiness, sadness, fear, anger, disgust, surprise, and 8 neutral clips). Each video clip has a duration of 2 to 5 minutes. Each participant completes 4 sessions, conducted on different dates. Each session consists of 20 trials. Each session induces 5 of the 7 target emotions to avoid frequent switching between emotional states. Each trial is divided into two parts: Viewing the emotion-inducing video (with a 3-second countdown before and after the video). Self-assessment of emotions: At the end of the session, participants review the video and provide a continuous rating (between 0 and 1). A rating of 1 indicates that the target emotion was strongly induced, while 0 indicates no emotion induction. For neutral videos, a score of 0 is given for emotional fluctuation, and a score of 1 is given for maintaining a natural state.
The raw EEG signal has a sampling rate of 1000 Hz and is downsampled to 200 Hz before feature extraction.

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG recording data into a standardized tensor format suitable for model training, covering the complete workflow from data loading, signal preprocessing, feature extraction, to label alignment. 1. Data Loading The specific load_data() function is used to traverse the specified directory by session ID (session_id) and participant ID (sub_id), directly reading and loading the data files stored in .npz format for reorganization. 2. Signal Preprocessing & Segmentation The raw EEG signals are first visually inspected for bad channels and interpolated using the MNE-Python toolkit. Then, a 0.1 Hz - 70 Hz band-pass filter and a 50 Hz notch filter are applied to remove low-frequency noise and power line interference. The sampling rate is downsampled from 1000 Hz to 200 Hz. The continuous signal is then strictly segmented into 4-second, non-overlapping time segments, each treated as an independent sample. 3. Feature Extraction & Smoothing From each 4-second segment, differential entropy (DE) features are extracted for five classical frequency bands (δ, θ, α, β, γ). Subsequently, the features are smoothed using Linear Dynamic Systems (LDS) or a moving average filtering method to further reduce noise. The resulting three-dimensional feature tensor (features) has the shape [number of samples, number of frequency bands (5), number of channels (62)]. 4. Label Generation & Synchronization The target emotion categories (7 classes) for each video, along with the participant’s subjective ratings (0-1), are extracted as label sources. The features and labels are strictly synchronized according to the time windows of the video playback, ensuring that each 4-second segment has a corresponding emotional ground truth. The final result is an aligned one-dimensional label array (labels).

This project provides a preprocessing method for the THINGS-EEG dataset, transforming raw EEG signals into model-ready data. The dataset contains high-temporal-resolution EEG responses from 10 participants to naturalistic object images, recorded using a Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm. Each participant completed 82,160 trials, covering 16,740 image conditions. The training set consists of 16,540 (image categories) × 10 (images per category) × 4 (repetitions), and the test set contains 200 (image categories) × 1 (image) × 80 (repetitions). Training and test stimuli were presented in a pseudo-random order, and target images were used to reduce blinking and other artifacts. EEG was recorded with a 64-channel EASYCAP (electrodes arranged according to the standard 10–10 system) and a BrainVision actiCHamp amplifier at 1000 Hz sampling rate, with online filtering (0.1–100 Hz) and referencing to the Fz electrode.

Acquisition Hardware: A 64-channel EASYCAP EEG cap (electrode layout following the international 10-10 system) paired with a Brainvision actiCHamp amplifier. Online Processing: During data acquisition, a real-time 0.1 Hz to 100 Hz band-pass filter is applied online. Reference Electrode: The Fz electrode is used as the online reference.

Core Paradigm: Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) paradigm. Stimulus Content: Object images presented against natural backgrounds. Quality Control Mechanism: “Target images” are interspersed within the image sequence to control participant attention and reduce the impact of blinking and other motion artifacts on the EEG signals. Experiment Scale: Each participant performs 82,160 trials (the training set includes 16,540 image categories, and the test set includes 200 image categories).

Raw EEG data from 10 participants. The raw EEG data in each .zip file is organized into four recording phases and training/test partitions according to the following directory structure: /sub-01 │ └───ses-01 │ └───raw_eeg_test.npy │ └───raw_eeg_training.npy │ ... │ └───ses-04 └───raw_eeg_test.npy └───raw_eeg_training.npy

This pipeline is designed to convert raw EEG data and corresponding stimulus image data into a standardized tensor format suitable for multimodal model training, covering the complete workflow from EEG signal preprocessing, image feature extraction, to feature and label recombination and storage. 1. Signal Loading & Epoching Using the core function epoching(), raw EEG data is converted to MNE format and subjected to channel selection. Continuous data is strictly segmented into time windows (epochs) from 0 to 1000 milliseconds following stimulus onset, with baseline correction using the data from 200 milliseconds before the stimulus. All retained electrode signals are downsampled to 250 Hz and categorized and sorted based on the image condition that evoked the EEG signal. 2. Signal Denoising & Whitening The mvnn() function is used to apply Multivariate Noise Normalization (MVNN) to both the training and testing sets. By calculating the covariance matrix of the EEG data and taking the square root of its inverse matrix (whitening matrix), the signal is transformed to eliminate inter-channel correlations. Additionally, for all EEG trials corresponding to repeatedly presented stimuli, averaging is performed to effectively improve the signal-to-noise ratio of the EEG image. 3. Image Preprocessing & Feature Extraction Stimulus images that induce emotion are spatially adjusted, uniformly scaled to a 224×224 pixel size, and normalized. The images are then input into the pre-trained CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) model’s image encoder to extract high-dimensional image visual feature maps. 4. Data Integration & Storage After processing with multiple scripts (processing.py, obtain_feature_maps_clip.py, processed.py), the preprocessed EEG data and image feature data are saved by participant (e.g., sub01), divided into training and testing sets, in .npz format. Upon loading, the EEG feature tensor with shape (number of samples, number of channels, number of time points), the image feature tensor with shape (number of samples, features), and the strictly aligned image category label tensor with shape (number of samples, labels) are readily available.

Cycle-GAN (Cycle Generative Adversarial Network) is innovatively introduced here as an unsupervised neural Domain Adaptation aligner. In the long-term application of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI), neural spike data recorded by microelectrode arrays often experience non-stationary drift in the feature space due to minor electrode displacements or natural neuronal evolution. Cycle-GAN breaks through the traditional limitation of having to rely on a large amount of labels from the target domain to retrain downstream decoders. Its core mechanism lies in: by constructing a bidirectional mapping, it seamlessly aligns the drifted neural spike features of the target day back to the feature space of the source day, thereby achieving calibration-free reuse of the pre-trained decoder. From the perspective of theory and code architecture, this Cycle-GAN alignment system consists of the following four key modules: Bidirectional Feature Generators The model instantiates two symmetrical generators, both adopting a three-layer Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) architecture, and integrating dropout and activation mechanisms. The generators are responsible for learning the non-linear mapping between high-dimensional neural feature manifolds, converting source domain features to target domain features, or restoring target domain features back to the source domain. Adversarial Discriminators The system contains two independent discriminator modules (also three-layer structures, outputting a single node), which are respectively responsible for distinguishing real neural features from the aligned features forged by the generators. By calculating the adversarial loss, the discriminators and generators drive the aligned features to approximate the true neural firing distribution in a minimax game. Cycle & Identity Constraints To prevent the generators from losing core kinematic or intentional information when mapping high-dimensional neural spikes, the model mandatorily introduces two key penalty mechanisms: Cycle Loss: Ensures that features can precisely reconstruct the original input after undergoing a dual mapping (from the source domain to the target domain, and back to the source domain). Identity Loss: When the generator receives intra-domain features as input, it constrains the output to remain unchanged, avoiding excessive distortion of the basal neural signals. Decoupled Downstream Decoders The system removes the coupling constraints between the aligner and the decoder. It provides a regressor (using a smooth loss) for fitting continuous kinematic signals (e.g., force, electromyography), and a classifier (using cross-entropy loss, with a built-in gradient clipping mechanism to prevent explosion) for discrete action/state recognition.

The system builds a rigorous staged validation and metric tracking framework. The core of model debugging focuses on the generalization evaluation of regression and classification: Multi-dimensional Regression Metrics: The model evaluates not only traditional average errors, but the most critical core metric is the multi-output variance-weighted coefficient of determination. To dissect 2D kinematic decoding more delicately, the code additionally splits and outputs the goodness of fit for independent coordinate axes. Classification Accuracy Monitoring: The evaluation standard focuses on strict classification accuracy. For data with multiple discrete categories, an increase in accuracy directly reflects the effective overlap of the feature manifolds. Periodic Validation & Checkpointing: The adversarial training process is highly unstable; therefore, after iterating a specific number of rounds, the system places the generator in validation mode and inputs the validation set. The system tracks the performance of the validation set after it is aligned and fed into the frozen source domain decoder. The generator and decoder weights corresponding to peak performance are saved as independent files. End-to-End Logging & Post-Processing: The framework features built-in logging objects and summarizes daily validation results into specific spreadsheet files. To adapt to further data analysis pipelines, users can also utilize scripts to losslessly map the data matrices (with headers removed) back into tensor files.

Relying on unpaired domain adaptation capabilities, this Cycle-GAN architecture precisely resolves the most acute pain points in the current field of long-term implanted BCIs: Zero-Retraining Reuse Constrained by microscopic changes in the physical environment of the brain, even the most advanced decoders encounter a performance cliff a few days after model calibration. This system ingeniously shifts the paradigm: instead of modifying the massive downstream decoder, it corrects the input data itself. By using the aligner to pull future shifted features back into the feature space of the initial training day, it vastly extends the lifespan of a single decoder. Overcoming the Label Scarcity Dilemma for Long-term Offline Data Cycle-GAN is essentially an unsupervised generative adversarial architecture (the calculation of core losses requires no involvement of true labels). This implies that, in real-world scenarios, patients do not need to spend hours each day repeating annotated calibration paradigms. The system relies solely on a small amount of unlabeled EEG sequences freely emitted by the patient to silently complete manifold space reconstruction and adaptive alignment in the background. Generalized Adaptation to Highly Heterogeneous Neural Tasks Whether performing continuous 2D force trajectory regression on macaque datasets or executing cross-week multi-intention pattern classification, this alignment pipeline demonstrates cross-paradigm universality. By simply replacing the trailing regressor or classifier, a unified feature manifold aligner can seamlessly drive a variety of diverse and heterogeneous downstream tasks. Offline Domain Adaptation & Online Decoupled Execution Cycle-GAN occupies a highly unique ecological niche within the system. Its highly time-consuming generative adversarial training and feature manifold alignment processes are typically strictly confined to execution on offline computing clusters or during cross-day pre-session calibration phases. However, because the architecture achieves perfect physical decoupling between the "alignment generator" and the "downstream decoder" by design, once the generator finalizes the weight solidification for cross-day manifold mapping, the data stream calibrated by real-time mapping can be fed into the downstream network with zero latency. This provides a pure, highly robust online real-time closed-loop operating environment for complex control tasks.

DFINE is a standalone regression and state inference framework specifically designed for processing complex time-series dynamical systems (e.g., neural population signals). Its core concept lies in combining the nonlinear feature extraction capability of deep learning with the rigorous mathematical derivations of classical linear dynamical systems (such as the Kalman Filter/Smoother), enabling dimensionality reduction of high-dimensional data while explicitly capturing the temporal evolution patterns of latent states, thereby achieving highly robust continuous trajectory tracking. From the code implementation and network topology, the present DFINE architecture consists of the following core modules: Multilayer Perceptron Feature Mapping Network (MLP Encoder, Decoder & Mapper) The model’s underlying layers do not rely on traditional convolutional or recurrent networks; instead, they adopt multiple sets of highly flexible multilayer perceptron (MLP) components. Among them, the encoder is responsible for nonlinearly projecting the high-dimensional raw observation sequence into a low-dimensional latent space (a_hat); the decoder is responsible for reconstructing the original observations from the latent states to ensure that core information is not lost; and the dedicated mapper network maps the smoothed internal hidden states into the final external behavioral or kinematic variables (behv_hat). Core Linear Dynamical System Module (Linear Dynamical Model, LDM) As the “heart” for capturing temporal dependencies, the architecture deeply integrates a differentiable linear dynamical model (LDM). This module is built with a rigorous state transition matrix (A), emission matrix (C), and learnable diagonal covariance parameters. It performs operations similar to Kalman filtering during forward propagation, and executes backward smoothing when do_smoothing=True is enabled. This mechanism allows the network to infer extremely smooth latent state trajectories that conform to physical/physiological dynamical evolution laws from noisy observational data. Multi-step Prediction and Multi-objective Joint Decoding Mechanism (Multi-step Prediction & Joint Optimization) In terms of optimization objectives, the model bypasses a single regression loss and adopts an ambitious composite optimization strategy. The loss function includes not only the observation reconstruction error (recon_mse) and the behavioral decoding error (behv_mse), but also innovatively introduces a K-step ahead prediction error (pred_mse, controlled by steps_ahead) based on the state transition matrix. This forces the model to learn an internal dynamic representation within the latent space that possesses strong forward-looking inference capability.

The debugging and evaluation of the DFINE model are deeply integrated within its dedicated training and validation loop (regressor.py): Evaluation Metrics: Multi-dimensional regression diagnostics: The system not only tracks the convergence of various MSEs in real time, but during the behavioral decoding evaluation phase it also rigorously calculates the mean squared error (MSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and coefficient of determination (R2 Score) in the original units. Among them, the R2 score on the validation set (best_val_r2) is established by the framework as the absolute core criterion for triggering early stopping and best-weight snapshot. Debugging Techniques: Mandatory Gradient Clipping: To completely circumvent the inherent gradient explosion problem during the joint optimization of the LDM and deep MLPs, the underlying implementation enforces clip_grad_norm_ before every optimizer step, strictly limiting the maximum gradient norm of parameters to a set threshold, which greatly improves the training stability of the model under low signal-to-noise ratio data. Step Learning Rate Decay and Dynamic Early Stopping (Step LR & Early Stopping): Combined with the Adam optimizer, the framework adopts a StepLR learning rate scheduler that periodically decays the learning rate according to the set epochs (e.g., decaying by 10% every one-fifth of the total epochs), effectively preventing the model from oscillating near the optimum point. Simultaneously, a tolerance-monitoring early stopping mechanism based on Patience is built in. Test-time Calibration: The framework innovatively supports online fine-tuning during the testing/deployment phase. Through the calib_scope parameter, researchers can finely control the unfreezing range (e.g., unfreezing only the shallow mapper or encoder_mapper). This allows the model to use a very small amount of calibration data (calib_ratio) for rapid parameter alignment, thereby preventing catastrophic forgetting.

Combined with DFINE’s code logic and input tensor assertions (which strictly require the regression target to have the shape (N, T, 2)), the architecture is primarily anchored in the following cutting-edge application scenarios: High-Fidelity Continuous Kinematic BCI Decoding: The model natively supports and focuses on processing two-dimensional continuous time-series labels (e.g., on-screen 2D cursor coordinates, robotic arm velocity vectors, etc.). With the smoothed latent states extracted by the LDM and the nonlinear mapping of the mapper, DFINE can effectively filter out high-frequency noise and abrupt changes in scalp EEG or intracortical microelectrode array (MEA) spikes, outputting extremely smooth and accurate motion control trajectories, making it an ideal decoder for neural prostheses. Latent Neural Dynamics Discovery and Reconstruction: The activity of neural populations is often governed by low-dimensional manifolds and hidden dynamical rules. DFINE performs nonlinear dimensionality reduction through the encoder, and the LDM explicitly fits the state transition matrix A. This makes it not merely a black-box predictor, but a computational microscope for neuroscience research, which can be used to uncover and visualize the underlying neural dynamical phase portrait as the brain executes motor intentions. Cross-day Non-stationary Rapid Alignment for Physiological Data: To address the most challenging problem of cross-day signal baseline drift in the BCI field, the model is built with multi-mode data normalization strategies such as day_mean_std. More crucially, thanks to the powerful Test-time Calibration functionality, it allows for ultra-fast fine-tuning of the mapper network using only seconds to minutes of calibration trial data before clinical deployment each day. This design balances the long-term memory stability of the core dynamics with the immediate flexibility of the surface mapping, significantly enhancing the system’s life cycle.

The Language LSTM is an end-to-end deep architecture specifically designed for Neural Speech Decoding and the analysis of high-frequency temporal physiological signals. In the field of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI), particularly under the "Silent Speech" paradigm, there often exists extreme Temporal Misalignment and highly non-linear long-range dependencies between the generation rhythm of neural spike sequences and the final output of linguistic symbols. Centered on the 11_Speech Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) theory, this architecture thoroughly breaks the reliance of traditional decoders on forced frame-level alignment. By combining the global temporal modeling capabilities of deep recurrent neural networks with the asynchronous marginalization inference of CTC, it achieves an autonomous mapping from highly noisy, high-dimensional neural dynamic manifolds to discrete semantic symbols. From the perspectives of theoretical mechanisms and network topology, this architecture consists of the following three core modules: Frontend Filtration & Manifold Unfolding Neural population activities are often accompanied by high-frequency transient Poisson noise. Instead of simply passing the signal through, the underlying SpeechFeatureFrontend of the model executes a rigorous set of physical preprocessing steps: High-frequency Noise Filtration: Utilizes the GaussianSmoothing module to perform one-dimensional convolutional smoothing on the input channels, filtering out high-frequency artifacts in the time domain while preserving the low-frequency population firing envelope. Cross-day Domain Shift Compensation: To address the physical displacement of microelectrode arrays over time, the model has built-in day_weights and day_bias parameter matrices. It performs linear calibration of the cross-day feature space directly at the input end via affine transformations. Receptive Field Reconstruction: Following Softsign activation, the nn.Unfold mechanism is employed to segment the one-dimensional temporal signal into overlapping two-dimensional windowed features. This operation significantly enriches the local dynamic context observable by subsequent networks at each time step. Deep Bi-directional Contextual Capture The inference core of the model relies on a multi-layer Bi-directional LSTM (Bi-LSTM) network. Unlike unidirectional evolution, the bi-directional architecture, when processing a complete single speech trial, can simultaneously converge historical firing memory and future evolutionary trends at the current time step. Coupled with the compact handling of variable-length sequences using pack_padded_sequence, the model can extract deep semantic trajectories hidden within lengthy neural impulses with extreme precision. Asynchronous Sequence Decoding & CTC Loss When the human brain formulates continuous speech, the length of neural firing is never equal to the length of the actual output characters. At the output end, the model maps features to the vocabulary space through cascaded LayerNorm and Dropout, and subsequently introduces nn.CTCLoss. By introducing a Blank Token and performing probability marginalization across all possible alignment paths, this mechanism endows the model with the robust capability to learn sequence mappings end-to-end without relying on any frame-by-frame labels.

In the domain of continuous language decoding, model evaluation must transcend simple frame-level accuracy and focus on the fidelity of the global sequence topology: Levenshtein Sequence Evaluation: The framework discards the rigid cross-entropy metric, adopting the Character Error Rate (CER) as the sole guiding north star. By calculating the minimum number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions (i.e., Levenshtein distance) between the model's greedily decoded sequence and the true semantic symbols, CER can most intuitively reflect the usability of the BCI system in real-world communication scenarios. Mandatory Dynamical Stabilization: When processing ultra-long neural spike sequences composed of hundreds or thousands of time steps, Bi-LSTMs are highly susceptible to arithmetic overflow caused by gradient accumulation. To thoroughly circumvent Gradient Explosion, the underlying optimization engine is hard-coded to execute strict clip_grad_norm_ (threshold 1.0) clipping between every forward/backward step, serving as a physical safety perimeter for model training. Adaptive Annealing & Generalization Defense: The system deeply integrates the ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler. When the model is trapped in a local saddle point on the error rate surface, the scheduler automatically performs learning rate halving annealing. Coupled with rigorous validation set state monitoring and the optimal model Checkpoint rollback mechanism, it perfectly intercepts the collapse of generalization capability caused by overfitting the training domain during the later stages of training.

The meticulous refinement of the Language LSTM architecture and its internal mechanisms directly addresses several ultimate challenges in modern closed-loop speech brain-computer interfaces: Breaking the Constraints of Strict Temporal Alignment: Traditional models based on Hidden Markov Models (HMM) or pure Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLP) require training data to possess exceptionally precise "frame-to-phoneme" level labels. In contrast, the Language LSTM leverages the marginalization capability of CTC, allowing researchers to directly utilize full-sentence text as target labels for training. This characteristic makes it possible to "directly generate natural language sequences from brain intentions," vastly reducing the manual labor cost of neural data annotation. Feature Extraction of Micro-Neural Dynamics: Raw, high-sampling-rate electrophysiological signals are replete with microscopic redundancies. Relying on the original SpeechFeatureFrontend mechanism, the model performs not merely noise reduction, but projects the time series into a higher-dimensional feature subspace via window unfolding (Unfold). This expansion of the local receptive field grants the LSTM extremely high sensitivity and fidelity when extracting weak cortical pronunciation commands. Combating Non-stationary Domain Shifts: The greatest nightmare facing implantable electrodes or scalp EEG systems is the drift of signal distributions over time. The parameter set provided by this model allows for flexible switching of alignment strategies between single-day and cross-day scenarios. In particular, disabling day_idx coupled with strong regularization provides researchers with a standardized and highly compelling testing tool to verify whether the features extracted by the model itself are merely "overfitted memories" or genuine "neural semantic invariants."

The Language Transformer is an end-to-end deep architecture specifically constructed for the analysis of high-frequency neural electrophysiological signals and continuous Neural Speech Decoding. In the field of Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI), particularly when confronting complex sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) mapping tasks, traditional recurrent neural networks (e.g., LSTMs) are frequently constrained by the serial computational bottleneck of time steps and gradient vanishing over exceedingly long sequences. This architecture thoroughly abandons the temporal recurrent paradigm under the Markov assumption, introducing a pure Global Multi-Head Self-Attention mechanism. Combined with the 11_Speech Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) inference framework, the model is capable of instantaneously capturing non-linear long-range dependencies between any two time steps across the entire neural sequence when processing a single speech trial (Single-trial), achieving parallel decoding from high-dimensional spatio-temporal dynamic manifolds to discrete semantic symbols. From the perspectives of theoretical mechanisms and network topology, this architecture consists of the following core modules: Frontend Filtration & Manifold Unfolding Neural population activities are often accompanied by high-frequency transient Poisson noise. Rather than simply passing the signal through, the underlying SpeechFeatureFrontend of the model executes a rigorous set of physical preprocessing steps: High-frequency Noise Filtration: Utilizes GaussianSmoothing to perform one-dimensional convolutional smoothing, filtering out high-frequency artifacts in the time domain. Cross-day Domain Shift Compensation: Incorporates built-in day_weights and day_bias parameter matrices to execute linear calibration of the cross-day feature space directly at the input end via affine transformations. Local Receptive Field Reconstruction: Following Softsign activation, it employs nn.Unfold to segment the one-dimensional temporal signal into overlapping two-dimensional windowed features. This operation significantly mitigates the inherent shortcoming of the Transformer in capturing local features. High-dimensional Projection & Positional Encoding Because Transformers inherently lack the Inductive Bias to process sequence order, the features output by the frontend are first aligned to a unified hidden manifold dimensionality (d_model) via linear projection. Subsequently, the model introduces Positional Encoding based on sine and cosine functions, seamlessly injecting absolute temporal rhythm information into the neural features, endowing the model with the ability to parse the temporal evolution laws of spike sequences. Deep Global Attention Encoder The core computational engine of the model is constructed by stacking multiple TransformerEncoderLayers. Multi-Head Attention: Allows the model to simultaneously attend to the temporal dependencies of multiple physiological dimensions, such as articulatory preparation, muscle activation, and acoustic feedback, within different feature subspaces. Norm-First Architecture: Explicitly sets norm_first=True. In exceedingly deep topological structures, this design has been proven to substantially enhance the backpropagation efficiency of gradients within Residual Connections, guaranteeing absolute training stability. Asynchronous CTC Decoding After the model output is mapped to the vocabulary space, nn.CTCLoss is introduced. Through probability Marginalization, this mechanism allows for end-to-end training between unaligned input spike sequences and target text sequences, thoroughly breaking the reliance of traditional decoders on forced frame-level alignment.
